name: Azure Service Bus API
what_it_does: Provides enterprise-grade cloud messaging via queues and publish/subscribe topics using AMQP 1.0 and REST, supporting reliable ordered delivery, dead-lettering, and transactions for decoupled distributed systems on Microsoft Azure.
use_cases: Decouple an agent's task dispatch from execution by posting work items to a Service Bus queue and having worker agents pull and process messages independently
Implement a pub/sub fan-out where a single event (order_placed) is published to a topic and multiple subscriber agents (inventory, billing, notifications) receive it via independent subscriptions
Use dead-letter queues to capture and inspect messages that failed processing in an agent pipeline for debugging and reprocessing workflows
Coordinate long-running multi-step agent workflows using message sessions to group related messages and ensure ordered, stateful processing by a single consumer
Bridge on-premises systems with cloud agents using Service Bus's relay and hybrid connection features for secure cross-boundary message passing
not_for: Ultra-high-throughput real-time event streaming at millions of events per second (use Azure Event Hubs for Kafka-compatible streaming instead)
Simple fire-and-forget webhook delivery to external HTTP endpoints (use Azure Event Grid for HTTP-based event push)
Non-Microsoft-cloud deployments where a cloud-agnostic messaging layer (RabbitMQ, AWS SQS) would reduce vendor lock-in
best_when: You are building Azure-native distributed agent workflows that require enterprise messaging guarantees — ordered delivery, dead-lettering, duplicate detection, and transactional message processing.
avoid_when: Your workload is primarily high-throughput event streaming rather than reliable message queueing, or you need cloud-agnostic infrastructure that can move between cloud providers.

auth.notes: Supports Shared Access Signatures (SAS) with configurable send/listen/manage permissions per entity, and Azure Active Directory OAuth2 with RBAC roles for more granular control. SAS is simpler; Azure AD is recommended for production agent deployments.

pricing.paid_tiers: Basic tier from ~$0.05 per million operations; Standard tier ~$10/month base + per-operation; Premium tier from ~$668/month with dedicated capacity and VNet integration
pricing.notes: Basic tier lacks topics/subscriptions and advanced features; most agent use cases require Standard or Premium. Premium is recommended for enterprise SLA requirements.

performance.rate_limits: Standard: up to 1,000 concurrent connections and 256 KB message size; Premium: up to 1 MB message size and higher throughput per messaging unit

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Azure Service Bus returns structured AMQP error conditions and HTTP error codes with detailed messages; SDK exceptions map clearly to service-level errors.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Built-in duplicate detection (configurable window) prevents duplicate message processing. Message IDs can be set by producers to enable exactly-once semantics.
known_agent_gotchas: Messages must be explicitly completed (acknowledged) after processing; failure to complete within the lock duration causes the message to reappear and be redelivered, which can cause agents to process the same message multiple times
Message lock renewal must be handled by long-running agents — if processing exceeds the lock duration without renewal, the lock is released and the message becomes available to other consumers
SAS tokens have an expiry and must be refreshed by agents on long-running connections; Azure AD tokens (preferred) also expire and require refresh token management
The REST API is fully functional but AMQP (via SDK) is strongly preferred for high-throughput scenarios — REST polling adds significant overhead versus AMQP push delivery

Topic subscriptions must be pre-created with their filter rules; agents cannot dynamically create subscriptions at runtime without Manage permissions, which is a broader permission than typically granted

security_score_components.security_notes: Azure AD RBAC with least-privilege roles (Data Sender, Data Receiver) is the recommended auth approach. SAS tokens can be scoped to individual queues/topics with send-only or listen-only rights. VNet integration available on Premium tier. Strong enterprise security posture overall.
